这个问题也和Web微信的一个BUG有关，具体表现为：
> 当从接口获取群列表时，会返回一些不存在的或者已经离开的群，但这些群的群成员为陌生人，或干脆为空的。

我们可以把这些不应存在的群称为: shadow group

因此我在 0.3.9.6 版本中增加了验证“群有效性”的逻辑，来过滤 shadow group:
> 在获取到群列表后，判断各个群中是否包含机器人自身，包含才认为有效

然而，我没有考虑到的是，在 wxpy 中还有两个受影响的逻辑:
1. 当尝试访问一个空群成员列表时(一些成员数量非常多的群会默认返回空成员列表)，会先请求更新这个群成员列表
2. 每当有新的群消息到达时，会尝试通过微信提供的 `FromUserName` 在机器人已知的群列表中进行比较，来判断来自哪个群

因此，当机器人中存在成员列表为空的 shadow group 时，wxpy 会在每次收到群消息时，去请求更新这些 shadow group。
因此高频的群成员列表更新请求，会导致微信返回 1205 错误，当然也会因此不返回我们本来需要的请成员列表。
而 itchat 中不会因为 1205 错误抛出异常，而是因为获取不到所需的成员列表而返回 -1001 错误: 找不到所需的群成员。

Yes, Extraterm is intended to be a full terminal application. It does contain an emulator component deep inside which is originally term.js. As mentioned above, I'm made quite a lot of changes. A simple diff between Extraterm's `term.ts` and `term.js` will most likely show a huge number of changed lines.

At the moment my prio is on Extraterm in general but not `term.ts` directly. It works quite well now and I don't have any major bugs which need to be fixed inside it. Future changes will probably be related to performance and of course fixing any bugs which are affecting applications. Implementing every last old obscure and unused terminal feature isn't a priority.

The point I want to make is that I'm happy with where `term.ts` is and don't have much motivation to go change that situation for Extraterm at least. If other people want to take and extract `term.ts` from the rest of the Extraterm code (probably not hard) and use it as a component, then they are welcome.

I'm not actually sure that Curio does this right now (in fact, it doesn't).   However, the original thinking on it was probably related to the fact that you can't do anything other than send `None` to a coroutine until it's run to at least the first yield statement.  So, it might have been a guard against that.

Thank you David

écrit :

> Although it's true that curio is a completely different thing than
> Twisted, the idea of some kind of interoperability isn't so far-fetched.
> One of the major goals of curio has been to remove the specifics of the
> underlying event loop from the picture (considering it to be a low-level
> implementation detail). For example, you don't normally interact with the
> underlying Curio kernel in any way other than through coroutines using
> await. In theory, it might be possible to swap in a different kernel
> implementation that would allow Curio applications to run on top of Twisted

(Quick paste-on-all-open-issues update:)

**TL;DR**: New Will release EOM. Also, you're awesome.

**Full Update:** This may be TMI for you, and if so, cool, the thing you probably care about is above.  But given the total radio silence on my part - and the fact that I've always tried to create a community here where we got to be actual people instead of avatars and PRs, I wanted to give a bit more context.

As you may be aware from an earlier AFK, I got married in December, which was great, but burned the month from a Will perspective.  I planned to get back on it at the start of January, but instead found myself selling our house, and simultaneously hit with a surprisingly severe bout of depression.  The time since Jan 1 has been sort of a blur.  A dark blur, but a blur more than anything.   I saw the PRs and comments coming in, but didn't have the energy or time to do anything about them.

Things seem to be clearing up on both counts now, and I'm _really_ looking forward to getting these fixes in, catching up on bug reports, and being active in improving Will again. :)

With that, I'm aiming to get all outstanding PRs bundled into a release, and all issues replied to by the end of February.

Most of all, thanks for your contribution to Will, your patience with an imperfect, quite human project maintainer, and for continuing to come back, engage, and spend some of the precious seconds you get on earth making this project better.

I appreciate you.  Thank you.

Hi Chris! 
Thanks for your input. You could call ```prune()``` and/or ```to_dict()```  before shipping it off to a module that doesn't handle the addict instances well. 

Now, here's the problem we're facing with returning something but a Dict. In addict the key feature, for me at least, is the fact that you can create nested dictionaries using attributes. 
```Python
>>> a = Dict()
>>> a.b.c.d.e = 2
>>> a
{'a': {'b': {'c': {'d': {'e': 2}}}}} 
```
This would not be possible (with the current implementation at least) if we were returning something else than an addict-instance, since when a unknown key is specified, we do not know if the intent was to set a value or getting one!

Does this sound OK to you?

Most of the complexity is because client.read is a low-level API entry point, and must be usable in many different situations.  Each different type of EtherNet/IP CIP device supports a different dialect of the protocol. so you must be selective.  The client.read API has to support this.

Is it a ControlLogix, CompactLogix (supports Read Tag [Fragmented], and routable requests)?  Then use the standard cpppo.server.enip.client APIs.  Also, you can use the Multiple Service Packet request to aggregate multiple requests into a single request.

Is it a MicroLogix?  Then, no Read Tag, no routable requests.  Use the cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute APIs.  No support for data types (eg. REAL); you have to compose the raw 8-bit SINT data into the desired data type.  Or, use the cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute proxy... classes to do it for you.

Others support only Read Tag Fragmented, others only Read Tag.  You just have to try them out, and see.  Others just have a broken EtherNet/IP CIP implementation (eg. PowerFlex "List Identity").

So, it's not a simple answer.  This is why the API is somewhat... flexible.  And, therefore, somewhat confusing.

There are several higher-level abstractions that you should use, if simplicity is a goal, and if you don't have any throughput challenges (eg. high latency).

Probably the simplest API to use is the cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute proxy APIs.

1) Try the proxy or proxy_simple class with your device, to see if it has support for "routable" requests.  Try something simple, like accessing the Identity object:

    from cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute import proxy, proxy_simple
    via = proxy( 'hostname' ) # try proxy_simple here...
    with via:
        vendor,product_name = via.read( [('@1/1/1','INT'), ('@1/1/7','SSTRING)] )

2) If that works, and you have a Tag you want to access, try:

    from cpppo.server.enip.get_attribute import proxy
    value, = proxy( '10.1.2.3' ).read( 'Some_Tag' )

These examples establish a connection, read the data and then close the connection.  Simple, but not efficient.  However, you can use the provided examples to improve things (eg. retain the connection, handle I/O failures, adjust pipelining to improve thruput, etc.), if you need to.

Cheers,

By "all this" do you mean [this](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/sonify.py#L211-L218)?  I believe it's because you coud have something like
```
Start | End  | Label
0.0   | 1.0  | C
1.5   | 2.0  | D
```
which you needed to convert to
```
Start | End  | Label
0.0   | 1.0  | C
1.0   | 1.5  | N
1.5   | 2.0  | D
```
Though I think we can avoid this now that you can pass intervals to `sonify.time_frequency`.

as of cdb789cd8590b748a68d8480765ee45c72da261b this appears to be working.  That is, relevant tests pass, and the previous test-case above works . . . specifically, this used to fail with an OSError because of too many open files, but now works:

```python
import pybedtools
import sys
a = pybedtools.example_bedtool('a.bed')
b = pybedtools.example_bedtool('b.bed')
for i in range(10000):
    sys.stderr.write('\r%s' % i)
    sys.stderr.flush()
    c = a.intersect(b, stream=True)
    len(c)
```

The fix is just on the iterator side, specifically in `IntervalIterator.__next__`.  See [relevant code here](https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/blob/d0c38c2b58ec3879b4cf2bacadcb98eb241483d4/pybedtools/cbedtools.pyx#L591). When the stream is being read from subprocess.PIPE, the pipe is closed when it's done reading.  An open file will also be closed upon hitting StopIteration; I don't think this latter case will cause any problems, unless the user wants to seek and read inside the file or something.

The part about this fix that I don't fully understand is that if you trap only the StopIteration in `IntervalIterator.__next__`, it still tries to call next() on the stream, even though immediately after closing a StopIteration has been re-raised.  This results in a ValueError because it tried to call `next()` on a closed file object.  Trapping this ValueError as well as the StopIteration seems to have fixed the original issue.  But I don't know why the first StopIteration isn't being propagated up to the `IntervalIterator` caller.  Couldn't find any documentation on `__next__` methods in Cython that discussed this, either.

It appears to work well for me though.  More testing prob needed before closing the issue.

rinohtype no longer supports the custom XML format that I started out with. The idea is now to support existing document formats by means of a set of frontends. ReStructuredText/Sphinx has the best support at the moment, but there are basic frontends for CommonMark, DocBook  and ePub too. A non-free [DITA XML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Darwin_Information_Typing_Architecture) frontend is under development. In the same way, it is possible to write a HTML5 frontend. It is probably a good idea to limit this to a subset of HTML5 that is better suited as structured text format such as [HTMLBook](http://oreillymedia.github.io/HTMLBook/). Contributions welcome!

rinohtype now also features a capable stylesheet system, which has been inspired by CSS. Please refer to the [documentation](http://www.mos6581.org/rinohtype/) for details.

As the author mentioned in a comment to another issue, Python will return exit code 1 if the scripts halts due to an exception. Furthermore as far as I know a renewal attempt is made upon each invocation of the script. So if exit code = 0, the certificate will have changed.

Note that the renewal script shown in step 6 does not check the exit code at all and will effectively erase the certificate if the script halted with an exception. So I highly recommend modifying it to check the exit code.

```Shell
#!/bin/sh

python /path/to/acme_tiny.py --account-key /path/to/account.key --csr /path/to/domain.csr --acme-dir /path/to/acme-challenge/ > /path/to/domain.renew
STATUS=$?
if [ $STATUS -eq 0 ]
then
    cp /path/to/domain.renew /path/to/domain.crt
    wget -O - https://letsencrypt.org/certs/lets-encrypt-x1-cross-signed.pem > intermediate.pem
    cat /path/to/signed.crt intermediate.pem > /path/to/chained.pem
    service nginx reload
fi

I'm trying to make sense of the behavior of channels.  From comments above, it's implied that a single channel cannot multiplex publications to a queue, because of the aforementioned write_lock?  I'm seeing the following performance behaviors specifically with regards to publishing:

1) If I try to create a new channel for the life of a single publish call, and discard it immediately afterwards, I observe fairly poor performance

2) If I create a single channel and a single exchange, global to all my various threads of execution, everyone uses it to publish, I see ~50% performance improvement

3) If I implement a fairly amateurish channel pool, and use a context manager to manage when the channel is "available" and when it's considered "in use", I observe performance very much like in 2.

If there were a write_lock, I would expect that some version of 3 should give me better performance.  I haven't seen that yet, though.  Its occurred to me that the write lock might have a much, much smaller lifetime than the "lock" imposed by my channel pool.

I guess much question is: is there a "best practices" method of managing channels and exchanges for an application with many threads of execution that all need to publish to a single queue as quickly as they can?

Hi Catherine, thanks for the detailed report. 

On first glance, I suspect the problem is that "cosmo" has been renamed to "cosmo_params" , and is not passed to the transfer_params. To do what you are trying to do, I would use the following:

```python
import hmf 
options={}
options['fname']="planck_for_hmf_transfer_out.dat"
mf = hmf.MassFunction(z=0, transfer_model = "FromFile", 
                                                   transfer_params=options,
                                                   cosmo_model="Planck15")
```

From version 2+, all components have two inputs -- a ``<component>_model`` and ``<component>_params``. The first is either a string (such as ``"FromFile"``) *or* a class (such as what you were using: ``hmf.transfer_models.FromFile``), and defines the overall model. The second is a dictionary within which are any particular parameters for that component model (so in this case, the dictionary should just be ``{"fname":"the_file_name.txt"}``).

The cosmology is no different -- the "model" here sets the basic model with parameters (actually, there is a slight difference with cosmology, because you can either pass a string or an *instantiated* object, but that's kind of hidden from the user). The "params" argument is again a dictionary with the keys being cosmological parameter names, and their values. These over-write the base parameters invoked in the model itself. Do note that sigma_8 and the spectral index (still called ``n``) are *not* part of the cosmology, and are passed as parameters directly to ``MassFunction``. 

I hope that helps, and let me know if you have any more problems!

I also note your point about the documentation being insufficient, and I hope to rectify that as soon as I can.

TestRanges introduced a new format I had not yet considered, a target containing multiple dates. It was trivial to call `resolveTarget` recursively on lists to handle this case:

```yaml
dates:
    cases:
        - target:
            - !replace 2006-08-29 xx:xx:xx
            - !replace 2006-09-02 xx:xx:xx
          context: 1
          phrases:
            - "August 29, 2006 - September 2, 2006"
            - "August 29 - September 2, 2006"
            - "08/29/06 - 09/02/06"
```

Since this is not a very common thing I decided not to add `startTarget` and `endTarget` fixtures. Instead, the tests just destructure the `target` list:
```python
@pdtFixture('ranges.yml')
def test_times(calendar, phrase, sourceTime, target, context):
    (startTarget, endTarget) = target

A quick hack to count COMMAND lines as if they were QUERY or WRITE lines is as follows:

```
diff --git a/mtools/util/logevent.py b/mtools/util/logevent.py
index ba74788..293a931 100644
--- a/mtools/util/logevent.py
+++ b/mtools/util/logevent.py
@@ -393,6 +393,13 @@ class LogEvent(object):
         if op in self.log_operations:
             self._operation = op
             self._namespace = split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 2]
+            if op == "command":
+                #"commmand <ns> command: <find/update/remove/etc.>"
+                cmd_op = split_tokens[self._datetime_nextpos + 4]
+                if cmd_op == "find":
+                    self._operation = "query"
+                elif cmd_op == "remove" or cmd_op == "update" or cmd_op == "insert":
+                    self._operation = cmd_op
```

I.e. it effectively makes 
"COMMAND [conn...] command <ns> command: find" lines -> "QUERY [conn...] find" lines,
and 
"COMMAND [conn...] command <ns> command: <insert/update/remove>" lines -> "WRITE [conn...] <insert/update/remove>" lines, at least so far mloginfo --queries shows.

Of course this leads to double-counting in the log files that have both the legacy QUERY/WRITE op lines and COMMAND lines.

Considering how to do the de-duplication effectively. The 'just-in-time' line and property parsing works against it so far as I can see at the moment.

# pysound_profile.py
import argparse

import tqdm

ap = argparse.ArgumentParser("pysound_profile")
ap.add_argument('filenames', nargs='+')
ap.add_argument('--overlap', '-o', type=float, default=1)
ap.add_argument('--block-size', '-b', type=float, default=6)
ap.add_argument('--hash', '-d', default='sha256')
args = ap.parse_args()

print("pysound_profile")
print(platform.uname())
print()

for filename in args.filenames:
    print("Processing '%s' with block size %fs and overlap %fs..." %
          (filename, args.block_size, args.overlap))
    with soundfile.SoundFile(filename) as sf:
        hasher = hashlib.new(args.hash)
        blocks = sf.blocks(
            round(args.block_size * sf.samplerate),
            round(args.overlap * sf.samplerate)
        )
        for block in tqdm.tqdm(blocks):
            hasher.update(block)

    print("%s = %s" % (args.hash, hasher.digest().hex()))

> I tested your example and I was getting varying times from 2.0 seconds and 2.1 seconds, but it would occasionally crash.

It is really weird, that your system behaves so differently. I'm starting to think that we're seeing two unrelated problems...

> The destroy function doesn't initiate the end.

I did not go by name: DriverProxy defines a `__del__` method, that calls `driver.destroy`:
https://github.com/nateshmbhat/pyttsx3/blob/cdeff8a5d9171245e4b2b48dc6065b407db328f1/pyttsx3/driver.py#L86-L90

And I could find no other cleanup methods defined.

> Could you see, if that solves your problem?

As far as I can tell, the `stop` method is normally not called, and is meant to finish talking early (otherwise it will return instantly). Also looping happens only during `runAndWait()`, which means my issue is outside of looping.

Nevertheless, I have tried and the results are the same. I also tried adding the line to `destroy()`, but got similar results to before. Actually, I don't think that field (`ISpAudio`) even exists in (my) sapi5 driver, though it only sometimes throws errors (...)

### But wait, there is more!

While writing this, I did spot a `close` method in the event handle that is returned from withEvents (`self._advise`), so I changed the destroy method of sapi5.py to:

```python
    def destroy(self):
        self._tts.EventInterests = 0
        self._advise.close()
```

This change achieved consistent 1.4-ish seconds execution time and still no crashes. _Incidentally_ this aligns fully with my original assumption, which makes me _want_ to like it (*cough ...)

Anyway, I still think this should be taken with a pinch of salt, all of this is very voodoo. Even though it does make sense, the basically non-existent documentation on most of the win32com stuff makes me _itch_.

Finally... I think I need to point out that I don't think my problem is a bug. The crashing was bad, but a random 5 seconds delay after the utterance is just weird and doesn't bother me. At this point I'm just curious of the weird behaviour ;)

The actual use case is input for a composite pattern. The reason I care is because in our application we have three input mechanisms, 1) directly through YAML, 2) through a "UI" which can generate the YAML, and 3) programmatic generation. Effectively we validate input -> generate corresponding data structures -> allow user configuration, and -> dump back to YAML. Since the current implementation of `voluptuous` generates reference duplicates, the final dump to YAML can become quite large and unwieldy. As a result, we currently can't use some features from `voluptuous`, such as defaults.

Sorry rather ridiculously delayed reply.  I don't think thread recycling is required.  A disconnect should result in the receiver_loop (effectively the run loop) completing, which would terminate a normal thread. The restart then creates a new thread and associates it with a new call to the receiver loop.  So under normal conditions, I don't think you'd ever max out the threads (although I guess there could be a gap in the code where receiver loop(s) never complete which means you'd exhaust threads at some point -- do you have a specific example?)

One gap might be thread pooling, if the client implements `override_threading` on the transport -- if there needs to be an explicit call to return a thread to the pool on completion.  That's something I guess could be implemented, but would need to see a example there to figure out if it's really a common pattern...

i noticed you're using heroku, which i believe uses gunicorn. gunicorn uses a pre-fork worker model where each fork actually shares the same memory addresses, but they in fact keep separate copies of data. since the queue manager is a module level variable, you'd be getting two separate copies, which is by design with a preform worker model. this is just off the top of my head and i could be wrong about your particular use case, but it sounds like what's going on from my naive vantage point.

Just as quick workaround, I went with a pagination approach of splitting my choices list into a multidimensional structure of chunks[][] that would fit in the terminal window, appending a Next/Previous Page entry and navigating through the chunks[][] if Next or Previous is returned.
It's not perfect, but it mostly addresses the problem for me.

`
import inquirer
from blessings import Terminal

def handle_large_data(numbers, listLength):
    p=0
    listoflists=chunks(numbers, listLength)
    for j in listoflists:
        j.append("NEXT PAGE...")
        j.append("PREVIOUS PAGE...")

    answer=select_an_option(listoflists[p])
    the_chosen_number=answer['number']

    while the_chosen_number is "NEXT PAGE..." or "PREVIOUS PAGE...":
        if the_chosen_number is "NEXT PAGE...":
            nextPage=p+1
            if nextPage >= len(listoflists):
                p=0
                print "Back to top"
                answer=select_an_option(listoflists[p])
                the_chosen_number=answer['number']
            else:
                p+=1
                answer=select_an_option(listoflists[p])
                the_chosen_number=answer['number']

        elif the_chosen_number is "PREVIOUS PAGE...":
            p-=1
            answer=select_an_option(listoflists[p])
            the_chosen_number=answer['number']

        else:
            return the_chosen_number
            break

def select_an_option(choices):
    questions = [
        inquirer.List('number',
            message="Pick a number:",
            carousel=True,
            choices=choices
        ),
    ]

    answer = inquirer.prompt(questions)
    return answer

def chunks(l, n):
    n = max(1, n)
    return [l[i:i+n] for i in range(0, len(l), n)]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create a list containing numbers 1-50
    choices = []
    for x in range(0, 50):
        choices.append( x )

    # Get height of terminal in characters, subtract 4 lines for display
    maximum_possible_list_length=(Terminal().height - 5)

    # If the amount of caretakers exceeds the height of the terminal, handle it
    if len(choices) > maximum_possible_list_length:
        the_chosen_number=handle_large_data(choices, maximum_possible_list_length)
    else:
        the_chosen_number=select_an_option(choices)

    print the_chosen_number
`

From [these lines](https://github.com/sergiocorreia/panflute/blob/master/panflute/tools.py#L224), it seems the YAML filters are more focused on the YAML part than on the a data part:

- It assumes that all content is YAML until `---` / `...`, so it doesn't allow opening rows (because they are not needed)
- If no `---` or `...` are found, it assumes that all the content is YAML (this explains your 2nd error)

The reason for this is that I was often working with external CSV files, so my markdown was usually:

```
Lorem ipsum

~~~ csv
source: somefile.csv
title: Some Title
~~~
```

Now, I agree that in your case and without a YAML part it looks uglier:

~~~ csv
---
header1 | header2
cell1 | cell2
~~~

If you think there's a way to improve the filter without breaking compatibility, I would be more than glad to add it.

Thanks very much for the pull request, and for your research into the issue: I actually didn't realise this incompatibility between Rabbit and AMQP even existed.

As a general rule, I think `asynqp` should _default_ to whatever RabbitMQ uses (since, really, no one's using anything else), even at the expense of compatibility with the AMQP 0.9.1 specification. Believe me, this situation does not make me happy, and I'm open to advice from anyone who disagrees with me.

I like your idea of having pluggable serialisation code for the various dialects of AMQP. That way we could provide an option to turn on "strict mode" and conform exactly to the spec, while still defaulting to "RabbitMQ mode". I guess this would be implemented as a set of classes defining overrides for the various differences between the various classes. I'd accept a pull request on this issue, though it seems like a fairly big refactoring - if you don't have time to put the work in then I don't mind picking it up, though it might have to wait a week or two before I get the time to put some work in. We'd also have to think about what the API for selecting a dialect might look like.

Regarding the content of this pull request in particular. From reading your [linked documentation](https://www.rabbitmq.com/amqp-0-9-1-errata.html#section_3), I'm not sure that the serialisation code you've included is entirely compatible with RabbitMQ. For example, the documentation states that Rabbit interprets `'s'` as a _signed 16-bit integer_, whereas [the code](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/pull/26/files#diff-c461f530b795067a1c99442a0969f714R89) suggests that we'd be treating it as a _short string_, in conformance with the specification. (I realise that it was probably me that wrote that in the first place; I suppose I would've been following the spec, not what was written in the docs you linked.)

So given all that, I'd prefer a pull request which implemented _full compatibility_ with Rabbit; then we can add strict AMQP compatibility back in later as a pluggable option. I also think that _perhaps_ version 0.4 of `asynqp` shouldn't include this change: I was hoping to put v0.4 out in a couple of weeks (as soon as I've found the time to clean up the error handling API, #23). However if you feel that releasing 0.4 without this change is not an option, I'd like to hear your arguments :smiley:

Here's an exerpt from the docs: http://pythonhosted.org/progressbar2/progressbar.bar.html#progressbar.bar.ProgressBar

> ```
> >>> progress = ProgressBar().start()
> >>> for i in range(100):
> ...     progress.update(i+1)
> ...     # do something
> ...
> >>> progress.finish()
> ```
> You can also use a ProgressBar as an iterator:
> ```
> >>> progress = ProgressBar()
> >>> some_iterable = range(100)
> >>> for i in progress(some_iterable):
> ...     # do something
> ...     pass
> ...

Yes, I forgot to mention that, casting to integer creates the step function. I think the most sensible solution would be to increase the `nugget` parameter of the GP object, which, essentially allows the possibility of observing noise labels.

From the documentation:
> Introduce a nugget effect to allow smooth predictions from noisy data. If nugget is an ndarray, it must be the same length as the number of data points used for the fit. The nugget is added to the diagonal of the assumed training covariance; in this way it acts as a Tikhonov regularization in the problem. In the special case of the squared exponential correlation function, the nugget mathematically represents the variance of the input values. Default assumes a nugget close to machine precision for the sake of robustness (nugget = 10. * MACHINE_EPSILON).

I have worked through this scenario, and I empathize because it was not easy to figure out. There are a couple things I had to do:

1. Bind the engine to your base's metadata

```python
engine = ...
Base.metadata.bind = engine
```

This engine might come from `FlaskSQLAlchemy` so it might look like:

```python
db = SQLAlchemy(app)
Base.metadata.bind = db.engine  # FlaskSQLALchemy wraps an engine and session
...
```

2. You also have to set the base's query, which can comes from a session:

```python
session = ...
Base.query = session.query_property()
```

In practice, this can also come from your `db.session`

```python
db = SQLAlchemy(app)
...
Base.query = db.session.query_property()
```

3. Run `Base.metadata.create_all()` for good measure.
4. User and Role can inherit from your declarative base and the FS mixins, no problem :)

`MockSet` by default has no knowledge of the objects' model. You can optionally specify the `model` parameter since not all functions need it. e.g.:

```python
    users = MockSet(
        User(**generate_fake_user_data(1, 'A')),
        User(**generate_fake_user_data(2, 'B')),
        model=User,
)
```

That should solve `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'DoesNotExist'`.

Also since it replaces/mimics Django's model query [manager](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/manager.py#L165), it raises `DoesNotExist` [like Django](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/query.py#L385) when getting an object by `pk`, which is what is [called](https://github.com/django/django/blob/35b6a348dea6b019679fe35fd443be875bdb028e/django/shortcuts.py#L93) by `get_object_or_404`.

Also when patching, in order to avoid patching every method (`all`, `filter`, `order_by`, etc.) individually, instead of:

```python
    user_objects = patch('app.models.user.User.objects.all', return_value=users)

    @user_objects
    def test_list_public_users_attributes(self, usersMock):
        ...
```

you can alternatively use:

```python
    user_objects = patch('app.models.user.User.objects', users)

    def test_list_public_users_attributes(self):
        ...
```

Hope this helps.

If the user doesn't use multiple inheritance, i.e. just `class Net(gluon.Block)` an exception will be raised if unknown parameters are passed. That is because Block inherits from `object`, passes these unknown parameters to the `object.__init__`, which will then raise an Exception. So the behaviour is essentially equivalent to the current implementation.

Consider however `class Net(gluon.Block, someOtherClass)`. Then the `super().__init__` call actually calls `someOtherClass.__init__`, so the behaviour depends on `someOtherClass`. But that is exactly what this PR is trying to achieve

Hello,

After searching a while, it seems that the ``riak.client.multi.MultiPool.__del__()`` method is not called when the error is **not** raised.

This is probably due to some circular references Python cannot solve so he is just not destroying the object.

When the threads aren't finished, they are still holding the reference to the ``MultiPool`` object (because the target is the method ``MultiPool._worker_method``).

The weird thing is that when the destructor is called, the ``threading`` module have in ``globals()`` everything that is not built-in set to ``None`` (probably due to Python exiting?).

When I'm closing a connection, I manually have to stop the pools:

```python
if client._multiget_pool:
    client._multiget_pool.stop()

if client._multiput_pool:
    client._multiput_pool.stop()

client.close()
```

In order to avoid the method ``stop()`` to be called again when the property gets deleted, I modified the ``riak.client.multi.MultiPool.stop()`` method:

```python
    def stop(self):
        """
        Signals the worker threads to exit and waits on them.
        """
        if not self.stopped():  # make sure the pool isn't already stopped
            self._stop.set()
            for worker in self._workers:
                worker.join()
```

And finally, I had to manually close the two pools instantiated in the module, in order to avoid the error at the exit of my program (I should use ``sys.atexit()`` for this):

```python
from riak.client.multi import RIAK_MULTIGET_POOL, RIAK_MULTIPUT_POOL

RIAK_MULTIGET_POOL.stop()
RIAK_MULTIPUT_POOL.stop()
```

Those are not bugfixes, just workarounds.

@ppmtscory I don't think you understand what this new EU VAT law is and if not please redirect this issue to someone else. Whoever in MTS that is responsible for the VAT articles would be good.

From Jan 1st 2015, when selling digital products to customers located in the EU the VAT must be charged at the location of the customer. This even applies to US businesses selling to EU customers.

The EU requires two pieces of non-contradictory evidence to locate a customer and the only ones that really apply to sales through the web are IP address and bank account location. If the IP and bank account mismatch then you can ask for a self declaration from the customer which basically means asking them where they intend to enjoy the product aka a "shipping" address. The self declaration (shipping address) is not a suitable piece of evidence by itself.

As it stands, it's not possible to solely use the REST API to legally sell digital products to the EU.

Additionally, the documentation states that no_shipping must be set to 1 for digital sales.

Module names are not unique; multiple packages can deliver modules with the same name.

Furthermore, `pipfile` shall provide a deterministic way to describe an environment; installing on demand defies the point because you introduce state. (How will one know what version of `django` will be installed when importing?)

```
import inspect
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from skimage import data

datasets = data.__all__[2:] # remove load and data_dir
number = len(datasets)

functions = inspect.getmembers(data, inspect.isfunction)
functions = [_function for _function in functions if _function[0] in datasets]

fix, ax = plt.subplots(4, 5, figsize=(20, 25))
plt.gray()
ax_ravel = ax.ravel()

for i, axx in enumerate(ax_ravel[:number]):
    im = functions[i][1]()
    if functions[i][0] == 'lfw_subset':
        im = np.block([[im[0], im[1]], [im[2], im[3]]])
    if functions[i][0] == 'stereo_motorcycle':
        im = np.hstack((im[0], im[1]))
    axx.imshow(im)
    axx.set_title(functions[i][0])

for axx in ax_ravel:
    axx.axis('off')

plt.show()
```

@contextmanager
def file_or_url_context(resource_name):
    """Yield name of file from the given resource (i.e. file or url)."""

@jefffohl this is little tricky. An OPF file will have columns by the `Model` used, if you take "anomaly detection" as the intended use-case for the visualizations, the model has to be `TemporalAnomaly` (which inherits from `TemporalMultiStep`) and always has an `anomalyScore` field. (+ the field(s) from TemporalMultiStep). 

A `TemporalMultiStep` will always have the `multiStepBestPredictions.actual` field, and depending on user's settings additional `multiStepBestPredictions.<N>` fields. 

There is also always the `timestamp`, but it may not have to be in a date format always, can appear as an iteration (1,2,3,4,...).

Other models are not actively used AFAIK.

This code is designed to be fast. It avoids using virtual methods in the "inner loop" code. The solution you've suggested would need to use virtual methods. For every iteration of `v: Neighborhood` it would check the vtable on the Neighborhood instance. In other words, we'd be sacrificing runtime performance to make the code cleaner (which, at the core, is what runtime polymorphism does). Sometimes maybe that's justifiable, but not in inner loop code.

I fully agree we need a way to install Invenio that doesn't break every other week because a new version of a dependency was released. Especially for new users who just want to try out Invenio this is important. 

Today, services are usually providing the requirements.txt file - see e.g. Zenodo. What makes it hard for Invenio is that have many "extra_requires" so one requirements.txt file likely won't do, and figuring out what precisely to put in the requirements is also not obvious due to Python versions, DB versions, ES version etc. Afterwards comes the pain of keeping this file updated.

I'd like to see Invenio becomes very easy to install and get started with, and part of that I think is providing different Invenio flavours (e.g. media archive, ILS, research data) that will actually be much like a service. I think that most likely the requirements.txt files can be added to those Invenio flavours, and we can have a say monthly patch cycle where the requirements.txt gets updated and released.

On Tue, Mar 11, 2014 at 5:46 PM, Tres Seaver <tseaver@palladion.com> wrote:
> -----BEGIN PGP SIGNED MESSAGE-----
> Hash: SHA1
>
> Background
> ==========
>
> In order to tune performance on large sites, I'd like to be able to
> experiment with different bucket / tree sizes.  For instance, we have
> sites where the number of loads for buckets is much too high for some
> kinds of requests:  increasing the bucket size could reduce the
> round-trip cost, as well as the likelihood of having the bucket evicted
> from the cache at all.
>
> Chris and I played around with this idea by hacking the constants in
> sandboxes, verifying that larger buckets seem to help reduce the load
> times.  They should also produce fewer un-resolvable conflicts (splits),
> which should help write performance under load, too.
>
> I pushed a branch[1] today which adds that configurability as
> constructor-only parameters,, e.g.::
>
>    from BTrees.OOBTree import OOBTree
>    tree = OOBTree(max_btree_size=500, max_bucket_size=1000)


Maybe I missed it, but I doesn't look like these parameters are being
passed to internal nodes.

>
> If you don't supply the values, they fall back to the current values set
> via '#define DEFAULT_MAX_{BTREE,BUCKET}_SIZE'.
>
> Unfortunately, as we went to work on the '__getstate__' /
> '__setstate__', Chris and I realized that there was no way to make the
> persistent state backward compatible:  BTrees and TreeSets store state
> as one of:
>
> - - None (empty tree / set)
>
> - - One-element tuple (tree / set with only one bucket)
>
> - - Two-element tuple (tree / set with multiple buckets).

This is an important optimization IMO, because it allows app
developers to be able to use BTrees for small collections without
worrying about the overhead of a second persistent object.
It's not mostly a matter of pickle size, but making BTrees reasonable
to use for small collections (that might need to grow to big ones).

>
> The overhead should be pretty minimal:  most of the frequent operations
> will still be implemented in C for the BBB wrappers.
>
> Any thoughts?

I've thought about this problem for a while and had always planned to
do the configuration at the class level:

- Add the configuration as class attributes.

  This means that you can do global experiments without recompilation
  and without updating application code.

  This avoids the __setstate__ issue.

  It also avoid having to plumb arguments through to internal nodes.

- Make BTrees subclassable.

  Then apps can create new BTree types that have different size
  configurations.  If instance dictionaries are disabled for instances
  of subclasses, this is probably easy.

  (It only occurred to me just now that subclassability is probably
  easy if you disallow instance dictionaries, which I think you can do
  with a flag in a type data structure.)

The downside of this approach is that it requires a class dictionary
lookup to get the sizes.  This might be mitigated by caching the sizes
on the BTree instances.  The cache would let you reuse a lot of what
you've already done.  Basically, rather than getting the values from
an argument list, get them from the class in __init__ and __setstate__.

I agree that the ``simple_problematic_query`` is supposed to return NULL for ?s, not x:jane. The query was oversimplified, but a more accurate ``simple_problematic_query`` would be:
```
select ?s ?o ?x where {
  ?x s:knows ?o .
  { ?x a s:Person. BIND(?x as ?s) }
}
```
*Now*, the result should be (?s=x:jane, ?o=x:john, ?x=x:jane).

Indeed, the group would return two bindings: (?s=?x=x:jane) and (?s=?x=x:john),
but only the first one would pass the outer scope, which constrains ?x further.

The same thing happens in the original query I proposed. So I claim that Corese and Virtuoso are not wrong :)
BTW, I checked with Corese: it passes tests bind07 and bind10, and it returns NULL for ?s with @joernhees ' original ``simple_problematic_query``.

The third method is the only one that is correct, since max_connections can only be changed [globally per cluster](https://patroni.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dynamic_configuration.html). Changing max_connections requiring restart of the PostgreSQL instance, which can also be accomplished  with `patronictl restart` (it's better to restart the replicas first).

BTW, *reducing* the value of `max_connections` may lead to the replica being unable to start if it has a lag. This is solved with in https://github.com/zalando/patroni/commit/e939304001dbce26b895a0cc22b72722b14e5ecd

Restore from backup, PITR and cloning of existing clusters task was floating around for a while and now the time to solve it nicely.

Following user stories are interesting for us:

1. We lost cluster completely and want to restore it from backup until latest wal segment available. Actually it's pretty much working out of the box right now. If the cluster structure is still valid in etcd all nodes will start recovering from backup and one of the nodes will grab the leader lock and promote.

2. We want to do PITR because somebody destroyed data in the database.

3. We want to make a full copy of existing cluster and run it as a separate cluster afterwards.

4. We want to do PITR but to a cluster with another name (Create `new-cluster` from the backup of `existing-cluster`). And after it reached a given time or transaction - promote and run as a separate cluster.

2, 3 and 4 actually possible even now, but requires a lot of manual work, i.e.: creating/removing keys in etcd, stopping and starting nodes, changing/reloading Patroni configuration.
Off course, everything has to be done in the right order and it's easy to make a mistake. 
Also it's not really possible to script it nicely.
The biggest problem here is - after restore is done, cluster must "forget" that it was doing a tricky restore.

Now I will describe the basic idea how can we solve it and what new functionality we need from Patroni. Right now Patroni only looks on `initialize` key in etcd.
If the `initialize` key is there and there is no data directory on the current node, Patroni will bootstrap from the master node (or may be from existing replicas) or from existing backup.

What if we introduce one more key in etcd, named `restore`. This key can contain some information needed for restoring from a backup, like recovery target settings and arguments for a restore script.

How it should work:
1. if there is no data directory and etcd doesn't contain `initialize` key, but there is a `restore` key defined, node must grab `initialize` key and run a restore script instead of running initdb.
1.1 After restoring from backup and having postgres running as a master it probably should change passwords for superuser and replication users.
2. If there are both (`initialize` and `restore`) keys are present - Patroni should wait for the `leader` to get pg_basebackup from it (no changes comparing to current behavior).

Basically `restore` key affects only initial cluster bootstrap process and tells that we should run some external script instead of initdb.

Having such functionality it should would become fairly easy to script all above mentioned cases.

I posted this comment https://github.com/grpc/grpc/issues/13906#issuecomment-355096277, but it might be worth posting here directly as well:

Our Linux binary wheels are built for `manylinux` platform, which is what PyPI expects. `manylinux`, for compatibility reasons, defines a very old kernel interface that does not support things like `epoll` system calls. Therefore, when we build the binary wheels to upload to PyPI, the `epoll` poll strategy will not get in the build and the default will be simple and more portable `poll`. The code path that seems to trigger the spinlock bug is on gRPC `poll` polling strategy. When you build from source locally on a modern Ubuntu machine, our more advanced polling strategies (i.e. `epoll`) get compiled in because your platform is known to support that and gets used by default, because it is supposed to be better, and it also seem to have the side effect of working around the spinlock bug.

    public final <Type> BuilderType setExtension(

        final Type value)
```

One common problem is that by default, ivy does not treat resolution of a pom and a jar as an atomic unit.  To turn this on you need to add `descriptor="required"` to each <resolver/> you want to act atomically, see the [common resolver atributes doc](http://ant.apache.org/ivy/history/latest-milestone/settings/resolvers.html#common).  With this resolver attribute turned on, a failure to download a pom for a jar dependency will fail the resolve and cache nothing in the ivy cache.  You simply re-run the resolve until success.  Without the attribute, the pom download failure would be silent and then the jar might download successfully, but with no transitive deps walked.  This tate would be cached by ivy and further resolves of the jar would continue to fail to add in transitive deps (only quicker now since the failure is cached!).

The resource models the AWS SDKs use right now don't support this yet which is why it isn't in the aws-shell, but I'd like to add this.

The two options I see are:

1) Denormalize the resources as you've done here.  This means that within the `elb` completions, you'd redefine the same [subnet in the EC2 completions](https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/blob/master/awsshell/data/ec2/2015-04-15/completions-1.json#L468-L473).  This makes the implementation simple because we know as soon as you type `elb` all the completions we need will come from a single file.  Also makes it easier to read these completions because you don't have to jump around multiple files.

2) Allow some way to reference resources defined in other files.  Something like:

in `elb/<api>/completions-1.json`:
```
"AttachLoadBalancerToSubnets": {
  "Subnets": {
    "resourceName": "ec2.Subnet",
    "resourceIdentifier": "Id"
  }
}
```

In this case, the completer would see "ec2.Subnet", load the completions for ec2, and then use the `Subnet` in `resources`.  Given that these files aren't going to be 100% auto generated from resource models, I'm slightly inclined for option 2 because it seems easier to manage by hand, e.g if there's a bug in the JMESPath expression for getting subnet IDs, we'd just have to fix it in one place.

Yeah, Bikeshed uses its own custom serializer. That warning from html5lib is on the *parsing* side, and it's because the HTML parser only recognizes a handful of "namespaced" attributes, and if you do something else you just get an attribute name with a colon in it, which is illegal per XML rules.

(I hate *hate* ***hate*** that particular error, as it gives *zero* information about what is being coerced, for no good reason. It's usually caused by forgetting to put a closing quote on an attribute value, so you end up with weird garbage. I've complained about this issue before to @gsnedders.)

We'll close this bug when the issue has been resolved.

Fair enough, though it does make comparisons slightly more complicated. For our use case we are managing a named container with a desired configuration by using the `create_container` function in the APIClient to create a temporary container, and then comparing the Config and HostConfig in the existing and temp container for differences, replacing the existing container with the temp if any were discovered. If a container was created outside of docker-py, you end up with one container having an empty list for a given value and `None` for the other.

However:

* I can see how trying to match the CLI defaults can be a pain in the butt, as any changes to the defaults in Docker itself will cause docker-py to drift from Docker. Playing whack-a-mole like this is no fun.
* It can be worked around easily by just doing a boolean comparison when one side is `None`. `bool([])` is the same thing as `bool(None)`, and the passing of any DNS options would keep them from being set to `None` in the created container anyway.

Given the above, and since this isn't considered invalid behavior, I'll go ahead and close this. I appreciate the feedback!

@ankurankan I also think that we should have base classes. But I think we should have a slightly different implementation road map. Here is what is think - 

We should have base classes but, instead of one base class we should have two
 **```ContinuousFactor```** and a **```HybridFactor```.**

Reasons for this choice,
 
- I think pure(continuous - continuous) relationships are different from hybrid(discrete - continuous) relationships. For example, in a discrete parent - continuous child relationship we will have different pdf functions for each state of the discrete parent. I don't understand how we can use a single pdf function to represent such relationships. (It would be great if you can help me on this).
- The ```HybridFactor``` class would be subclassed by classes like ```CanonicalTable``` which would itself be using classes like the ```Factor``` class (the one we already have) and ```CanonicalFactor``` (the one that I think should be subclassed by ```ContinuousFactor```) . (Although I am not 100% sure about its implementation, but it will be something having Factor tables with CanonicalFactor objects as the table contents). Keeping ```CanonicalFactor``` and  ```CanonicalTable``` derived by the same base class does not make much sense to me. 

I believe by keeping the two families separate we can generalize things in a more efficient way.

Now, if we do have two separate base classes, there could be two cases,

- We will be discretizing the continuous distributions to obtain  ```Factor``` objects and then applying inference, like the way I discussed on my blog post.
- We will be directly using these classes to perform inference tasks. For example, we will be using the ```JointGaussianDistribution``` class to perform Hamilton sampling etc. and the ```Canonical Factor```  or the ```CanonicalTable``` class to perform the sum product inference tasks.

Now our base classes should handle both these cases and they will. These classes will behave exactly the way you suggested in your previous comment and so will their subsequent derivatives.

Earlier I was planning to have separate classes for the base ```ContinuousFactor``` class and a helper class providing the discretization functionality. Hence, I was keeping this class restricted to one variable. But now I think the ```ContinuousFactor``` class can also behave as a helper class in various places. Hence, I will implement it the way you suggested.

As for the other parts of the implementation road map, please let me know if you think differently.

Ok, agreed: `PUBLISH`/`PUBLISHED` and `EVENT`/`EVENT_RECEIVED`, the latter with the semantics 1) is appropriate and sufficient for QoS 1 (but not QoS 2!).

> QoS 2 needs to tie into the app code to achieve the two-phase commit, transactional semantics. Eg think about receiving a "charge event" that deducts money from an account. You want to process such events *exactly once*, where "exactly once" extends right into the transactional database. A client that really does QoS 2 will need a local persistent storage (better database).

I also think `EVENT_RECEIVED` is appropriately named. QoS 1 is one mode of assured delivery. QoS 2 another. I have to re-read the MQTT spec parts to see if it is really "transactional messaging" in the hardcore, ACID database sense (there are transactional messaging system .. I just don't know if MQTT is one of them).

The parent process interprets SIGINTs and SIGTERMs as shutdown requests. Commit fd4146d fixes the problem where the Manager process can exit before the others which then causes them to crash.

The parent process also has a SIGHUP handler set up and interprets this signal as a request to reload its config. That seems to work fine.

That said, I do agree that the signal handler setup code should be refactored so that it's all done in the same place and using the same mechanism. However, to fix/close the issue described here, combined with fd4146d, the service file should be modified so that a 'service stop' should only send a SIGTERM to the main process.

I've started playing around with this and should hopefully be in a position to make another PR soon.

#3822 should fix this behavior on upgrade by giving the dirs a TTL of 0, meaning "never expire".

Oh, no, I'm looking at linux-64. 

I'm re-opening this because I think we should talk about the problem---I'm not convinced conda is wrong here---or at least, there are reasonable interpretations. 

The problem is that `gdal`'s dependency lists are all over the map. Conda tries to differentiate between "strong" and "weak" dependencies: "strong" dependencies are shared across _all_ versions of a package, while "weak" dependencies are version-specific. In the case of "gdal", it's quite crazy:

- The earliest versions depended only on `python`
- Later versions brought in `numpy`
- Later versions brought in `libnetcdf` and `hdf5`
- Somewhere in the _middle of version 1.11.2_, `libgdal` was brought in. That's right, `libgdal` isn't even a consistent dependency in a minor version of `gdal`.
- _For build 1 of version 2.0.0_, a bunch of the dependencies are _dropped_, and _all_ that is left is `libgdal`, `numpy`, and `python`. Furthermore, the version requirement in `libgdal` is _dropped_: according to the metadata, any version of `libgdal` will do. (Surely that's not right.)

This is crazymaking for a solver that is trying to take an algorithmic approach to determining which packages should be brought in and which should not be. Currently, the solver maximizes versions in this order: 1) requested packages, 2) strong dependencies, and 3) weak dependencies. Furthermore, when maximizing weak dependency versions, it favors _no package at all_ whenever possible. So the solver likes the idea of not having to bring in a few extra packages.

So my view is this: if a particular build of `libgdal` is critical here, then it needs to be _explicitly requested in the dependencies_. If you're not doing that, then you're giving the solver the discretion to do things you might not like.

Conda 4.3.x doesn't know anything about `base`.  In 4.3.x it's `root`.  In conda 4.4.x it's `base`, but conda 4.4.x also treats `root` as an alias of `base` for backward compatibility.

So `RUN conda env update -n base` will NOT work for conda 4.3.x, while `RUN conda env update -n root` will work for both, and work in the same way for both.

It's not security related. If you pass `validate=True`, then `boto` runs a query on the bucket before creating the object it returns to you. If `validate-False`, it just returns the object. Relevant part of the `boto` code: https://github.com/boto/boto/blob/2.39.0/boto/s3/connection.py#L465L550

When setting `validate=False`, you should also remove `bucket = conn.get_bucket(bucket_name, validate=False)` from the try/except, since it would now never trigger an exception.

ISTM that this request is not conceptually possible to fulfill. If, e.g. I have `env1.yml` and `env2.yml`

```yaml
name: py3
dependencies:
  - python=3
```

And:

```yaml
name: py2
dependencies:
  - python=2
```

These cannot be merged automatically.  Even if they did not specify incompatible tool versions, the *name* of the environment is different in each file.

I believe this Issue should be adjusted instead to request the creation of a separate `merge-conda-env` tool that guided creation of a compatible superset environment.  Of course, such a tool does not necessarily need to live in this repo, it could be independent.

@nitzmahone Indeed, there is two types of packages:
1- Multi-api packages. Currently there is 5: storage, network, compute, resource, containerregistry. These packages have:
    - A `models` method on the client, that takes an ApiVersion as parameter
    - An `api_version` parameter in `__init__`
    - A `profile` parameter in `__init__`
2- Single-version package, that have no `models`, no `api_version`, no `profile`.

All of them should have a `models` attribute in operation groups.

Then you should:
- Check for `models` in the client to determine if the package is ready for multi-api. No need for inspect. You can also check if `DEFAULT_API_VERSION` or `DEFAULT_PROFILE`  is in the client.
- Always use `models` on the operation group directly. Since this will stay the same once/if the package goes to multi-api one day.

In real life, packages released since two months have the `models` on the operation group, but not the older. I would suggest that you tell me "I need package XXXX with `models` on operation group" if you integrate faster than I release. So I release a bugfix version adding that, to simplify your life.
Same for multi-api, I think resource and containerregistry doesn't have profile yet, but the new resource is planned for today, and I can release containerregistry with `profile`. So you can simplify your factory.

Thoughts?

Some graphics systems are Y-up, some are Z-up. In most cases it's easy to change, so use whichever feels natural. Just make sure the graphics subsystem, the physics subsystem and the model assets use the same setting.

Both make sense depending on where you come from. If you draw a graph on paper, the X-axis goes to the right and the Y-axis goes up. But if you draw a world map and try to map it to the real world, the X-axis goes across the land in one direction and the Y-axis goes across the land in another direction perpendicular to the X-axis. Now if you want to express elevation, all you're left with is the Z-axis, so you end up with Z pointing up.

More especially, IPython will consider the return value of the ast expression of the Abstract Syntax Tree as the output value for current cell. In a notebook it might be difficult to see but in a pure IPython shell you can see the difference between printing and returning:

```
In [1]: 1
Out[1]: 1

In [2]: print(1)
1
```

In the second case you do not get a `Out[ ]`.

IIRC the distinction is on purpose as Python repl does not allow multiple statement:
```
>>> for i in range(5):
...     i
... 1
  File "<stdin>", line 3
    1
    ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

while IPython does:
```
In [1]: for i in range(5):
   ...:     i
   ...: 1
   ...:
Out[1]: 1
````

And doing same behavior than Python repl too often flood the screnn.


As you are using python 3 and generator be aware that `range()` is already a generator [technically more that that but not the goal here]. So your `(x for x in range(5))` is basically reimplementing range :-). Though it is a nice start to work with generator !

I have been building presentations with ``nbpresent`` for data-driven interactive documents.  My first lesson was that too much code makes creating a presentation from a single notebook unwieldy.   

There needs to be a tighter way to connect variables on the kernel with  HTML and Javascript.  I came up with this small cell magic called [``literacy``](https://github.com/tonyfast/literacy).  `%%literate` processes the cell as literate markdown where the code fences are executed if the language is understood.  Moreover, the entire cell is a jinja2 template, well a few templates, which means that data can passed from the kernel to the presentation and made interactive.

[This notebook](http://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/tonyfast/3d80ffaa8a2c5d04d3d2cd7631a7cb19/hard%20to%20find%20a%20better%20way%20to%20interact%20with%20javascript.ipynb) showcases ``literacy``.  Some cool features are:

* Markdown cells that create variables in python and insert them into the template.
* Coffeescript, javascript, and pyscript to change state.
* A DataFrame inserted directly into markdown after making a request.
* Hidden code cells and disqus comments.  _Click the toggle input button to show the source._

>>> approach to handlers that Tornado took. I then heard from Flask users that
>>> they liked just putting a decorator on functions, so I added support for
>>> that too.
>>>
>>> Because everything else was the same, I was able to directly test the
>>> performance of Tornado's class style vs. straight functions. In Tornado,
>>> the handler is a class. Not a class instance, but a class. The instance is
>>> created for each incoming request. Additional overhead is that the class
>>> supports HTTP GET by implementing `get()`. For each request, the class is
>>> checked to see if it has implemented the function that would exist for the
>>> incoming HTTP method. HTTP POST = `post()`, etc.
>>>
>>> We implemented the test with a straight-up hello world test. A very
>>> basic request kept the differences between using functions and classes
>>> minimal. The results was that functions would handle about 133 requests for
>>> every 100 the classes could do.
>>>
>>> Further, the reason I first liked Schematics being as instance free as
>>> possible, is that I could use Schematics models for building API endpoints
>>> and could do so without instantiating any models. No handler classes and as
>>> few Model classes as required to get the metaprogramming to work. For this
>>> case, the dict was often API inputs and I'd validate / transform this data
>>> based on what model the handler was built around.
>>>
>>> It's been such a long time since I used Brubeck... I think I might've
>>> put the results on my tumblr, which no longer exists.
>>>
>>> Accessing plain dictionaries is obviously faster, but in the context of
>>>> Schematics this only accounts for a fraction of the CPU costs. And any such
>>>> performance difference could be optimized away by making the transformation
>>>> loops aware of instances and having them access _data directly.
>>>>
>>> Anyone can put Schematics in a loop though.
>>>
>>>> When importing, the resulting (internal) data structure is a
>>>> newly-populated dictionary in any case. So literally the only difference
>>>> between returning a dictionary vs. a model instance is the creation of the
>>>> model object, which doesn't seem very expensive in the grand scheme of
>>>> things.
>>>>
>>>> Hence, it's my belief that, if there are any notable performance gaps
>>>> between plain dictionaries and model instances, they are merely design
>>>> issues that can be fixed.
>>>>
>>>> One scenario where, historically, there has been a significant
>>>> performance penalty is basic validation. Passing a dict to validate()
>>>> is efficient because both conversion and validation are performed at once:
>>>>
>>>> validate(MyModel, inputdict)
>>>>
>>>> Doing the same thing using an instance involves importing first and
>>>> validating after:
>>>>
>>>> MyModel(inputdict).validate()
>>>>
>>>> Since validation also has the conversion step baked in, the whole
>>>> import process is run twice for no reason.
>>>>
>>>> This is addressed in PR #374
>>>> <https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/374>, which improves
>>>> the performance of the above scenario by ~40%. The functional variant is
>>>> now only about 3% faster than creating and validating instances.
>>>>
>>> This is good news.
>>>
>>> Memory consumption is another consideration when using instances. Python
>>> classes are built on top of dict's, which all must have considerations on
>>> top for growing memory structures, and all must have unique allocations.
>>>
>>> Class instances, by nature, require more resources.
>>>
>>> Alas, Python isn't the fastest language out there.
>>>
>>>> Btw, Kalle - you're doing a goddam great job with all your Schematics
>>>> work. I have only been speaking up when I have something different from you
>>>> to say, which means you don't hear from me too often.
>>>>
>>>> Thanks! Good to hear. I've been debating whether to push forward with
>>>> my plans for 2.0 or make it a separate project, simply due to the lack of
>>>> community participation here on GH. I don't want to make too drastic
>>>> changes if neither users nor maintainers take a stance one way or another,
>>>> so it's important to know people are at least watching what goes on.
>>>>
>>> It's an open source world out there. You're free to do whatever you like.
>>>
>>> But there does seem to be a network of users around Schematics and so
>>> far you've been making the recent changes, so hopefully you feel your
>>> opinions are well represented inside the code base.

I gave a solution to this over at #257 which I think is good advice for folks in general: if a function is not yet built into `autograd` you can almost always build it yourself.  Thats the beauty of the tool.

You can define. `np.cumprod` by extending autograd by defining your own primatives (as nicely detailed in the [autograd tutorial document](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/docs/tutorial.md)).  But this requires that you yourself understand how to implement its gradient.  

If you don't want to have to figure that out - take the easy way out (I often do):  define the function (here `np.cumprod`) whose gradient you are interested in computing using components that `autograd` already knows how to differentiate.  So -  in other words - create your own `np.cumprod` function using simple looping and `numpy` functions that are already built in, and then `autograd` can compute its gradient right away.  I built a version of `np.cumprod` over in  #257 that runs virtually just as fast as the original (for the example data provided here) and `autograd` can differentiate it just fine.

The implementation does in general not support to use a population which is not generated by `ask`. The result could be completely unpredictable. I can't see what you are trying to achieve. After `tell` is called, there is no population anymore in the algorithm, so you can just give the weighted mean of the initial population from `generate_pop_init` as initial point. Specifically, 

```python
es.mean = dot(es.sp.weights.positive_weights, 
    [X[i] for i in np.argsort(fit)][:es.sp.mu]).T
```
should replace the first `ask` and `tell`. But then, this could also just be used as first argument to `CMAEvolutionStrategy`.

Rate limits are enforced on a "TLD + 1" level based on the public suffix list (including private suffixes, from how I understand the code).

Dynamic DNS providers can request to be added to this list [here](https://publicsuffix.org/submit/).

You could
 * try contacting no-ip and check if they're willing to add their domains,
 * switch to a provider already on the list (at a glance: DynDNS, probably others too),
 * or wait for GA, where rate limits will probably be relaxed a bit, and rush to get a cert :smile:

So turns out it does work. The problem I was having was in how I was checking for a timed out poll:
```
    while True:
        msg = c.poll(timeout=2)
        if not msg:
            continue
        ...
```
I should have been checking for None explicitly, i.e.,
```
    while True:
        msg = c.poll(timeout=2)
        if msg == None:
            continue
        ...
```
So my bad. Although I wouldn't have expected 'not msg' to evaluate to true just because there is no message value (and I'm not really clear on why it does).

An alternative approach is to pass an optional `timezone` argument into `rrulestr`:

* If both `START` and `UNTIL` are tz-naive, it is unused.
* If both `START` and `UNTIL` are tz-aware, it is unused.
* If either of both is tz-naive, and the other is tz-aware, then set the timezone for the naive one to the value of the `timezone` argument.

This allows downstream users to "pick" between either (1), (2) and (3), but also keeps the GIGO default (eg: when no `timezone` is passed, keep the current behaviour), which really makes sense as the default behaviour (as has been mentioned earlier on this discussion).

---

The big downside I'm seeing it that timezones that come from `pytz` (I think we can all agree this is probably an extremely common scenario) can't be used like `dt.replace(tzinfo=tz)`, but rather need to be used like `tz.localize(dt)`. But a try/catch will probably fix this.

About the "expectations".

pySerial uses `select` on POSIX and expects that when it signals "ready-to-read", that either actual data is available or otherwise its interpreted as fatal fault and thus an exception is raised. Early versions of pySerial just did read again and then you'd end up in 100% CPU load when someone unplugged the USB-Serial adapter. Not a good thing and no chance for the application to do anything about. Now you have the exception and can decide in the application what to do, e.g. close and try to reopen the port.

Actually `poll` would have nicer properties, such as separate flags for "ready-to-read" and "error". I tried to use that one but it turns out that some fruity OS and hardware vendor decided to not support that part of POSIX... and I rather have the same behavior on all POSIXy systems that have an other differently behaving implementation. But if someone wants to try `poll` on a system that actually implements that call (such as Linux), you can try it out with [`alt:///dev/ttyUSB0?class=PosixPollSerial`](http://pyserial.readthedocs.io/en/latest/url_handlers.html#alt)

Thanks for trying to move the ball on this Mark. I rember Bishop’s paper from my NREL days! One of the first papers that I read on the single diode model (SDM).

FWIW, for my own SDM i-from-v and v-from-i calculations, I have resorted to using Scipy’s Newton solver using an explicit ideal diode calculation for initial condition. (I currently use the solver in Halley’s method mode, but I’m not sure that’s better overall given the extra second derivative calculation cost.) One downside to the canned solver is that it is harder to cache parts of the calculation that can be reused. I have also been able to “vectorize” the canned solvers, but I haven’t determined the overhead for this either.

I like straight up Newton solving because the logic around handling ideal (or near-ideal) devices is much more straightforward than LambertW approaches. (It also _seems_ to translate well to the corresponding double-diode model calculations, making for a more consistent code base.) In an earlier pvlib PR of mine, the question was raised about using the residual calculation on the sum of currents at the diode node in the convergence metric as opposed to the LambertW approach. but why?

Added in extension to arrays as Z3 already treats arrays as function 
spaces (using the as-array operator).

Two examples:
https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3test/blob/master/regressions/smt2/ho.smt2
https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3test/blob/master/regressions/smt2/memset.smt2

Support is very rudimentary, and mostly lambdas are syntactic sugar, which may be adequate.
The ho.smt2 example illustrates one capability of extensional reasoning that goes slightly beyond the
realm of marcos.

Internalization prior to search performs a closure conversion, such that (lambda (bound) (body bound free)) 
is represented as nLambda(free), and the equation:
 (forall (bound) (= (select (nLambda free) bound) (body bound free)))
The equation is tagged so that MBQI doesn't get trapped satisfying it.

This is a pity. We are using the interpolation of Z3 in Ultimate Automizer and there are verification problems that we can only solve in a setting where we use the interpolants of Z3.
I have to admit that it run not always smoothly, but this was not a problem for us. In case Z3 failed, our tool just tried something else.

Shameless self-plug: As an alternative to interpolation in software verification we implemented an unsat-core-based version of an old approach from the Newton/Slam toolkit.
The [evaluation of our FSE 2017 paper](https://dl.acm.org/citation.cfm?id=3106307) shows that the unsat-core based approach can (at least for our tools) be an alternative to interpolation.

So, it would be nice if we could get the interpolant support back some time and it is vital for us that you never drop the support for unsat-cores.

Hi @Alonmeytal 

This is happening because the script is saving frames inside a collection. 
At the moment, all frames are recycled and live within shared frame-pools. By default, we only allow for 16 frames to cycle in the system. 

This is done to reduce allocation time (we just fetch frame from the pool) and to ensure there is no consistent resource leak (if frames are processed slower then the rate at which they arrive, you must implement some kind of throttling mechanism or the application will run out of memory very fast). 

We do however acknowledge the requirement to be able to save unlimited number of frames (either for data-collection or in garbage-collected environments) and will enable it soon. For now, if you are building the library from source, you can build the `development` branch and replace `option_range{ 0, 32, 1, 16 }` with `option_range{ 0, 32, 1, 0 }` in [source.cpp:41](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/development/src/source.cpp#L41). After rebuilding (with `-DBUILD_PYTHON_BINDINGS=true`) the script should work.

Hello @matthiasplappert , I have tried to implement this [paper](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.10089.pdf) on "Overcoming exploration with demonstrations in RL" Ashwin et al. over the HER baselines and got improved results and faster convergence with the toughest of the Fetch tasks, Pick and Place by training the agent with demonstration data. 

The changes do not affect the original execution of the code in any manner. The only changes that need to be made to the code to run with demonstration data is some parameters in the config.py file, where I have introduced additional parameters and provided proper description alongside. Description provided in the README file.

Functionalities implemented from the paper - 
* Demonstration Buffer : A separate buffer is created and initialized with demonstration data.
* Behavior cloning loss : The loss functions are modified with the addition of behavior cloning loss as an auxiliary loss on the Actor's outputs.
* Q-filter : Selecting only the updates from the demonstration examples which actually improve on the Q values.

Execution - The results are reported with a configuration as described in the README file, and are trained with ```--num_cpu = 1``` . Reproducing the results is simple, instructions are given in the README. The original configuration is kept intact such that there are no problems when training with vanilla HER.

Results - Training with demonstrations helps overcome the exploration problem and achieves a faster and better convergence. The graph presented in REAME contrasts the performances of training with and without demonstrations.

```
def main():
    env = gym.make("CartPole-v0")
    act = deepq.load("cartpole_model.pkl")

    while True:
        obs, done = env.reset(), False
        episode_rew = 0
        while not done:

            obs, rew, done, _ = env.step(act(obs[None])[0])

`act(obs[None])[0]` - is giving selecting action with the highest Q value for the current enviroment state
`obs, rew, done, _ = env.step(...)` - is performing the action. It returns the new enviroment state (obs), any reward (rew), if the enviroment is terminated (done) and debug info (_)

Before saying how field tracker works, let's see how Django internally handles pre-save and post-save (I recently learned this in a related issue)

```python
def save(self, *args, **kwargs)
    print "Before Model Save"
    // Super call
    print "After Model Save"
```

Now let's add one pre-save (which print `Pre-save`) and one post-save (which prints `Post-save`) signal to this model.

Now, when we execute the save method, this would be the order of execution:

```
Before Model Save
Pre-save
Post-save
After Model Save
```

This means, Django first fires all post-save signals and then execute rest of Save function! (An interesting implication was covered in [Pickle-Issue 83](https://github.com/jazzband/django-model-utils/issues/83))

********

Now, with this understanding let's see where field update actually takes place.

Looking for relevant code for Tracker, here it is:

```python
def patch_save(self, instance):
   original_save = instance.save
   def save(**kwargs):
      ret = original_save(**kwargs)
      // do_some_updates
      return ret
   instance.save = save
```

So, yes, it looks like Field Tracker updates save method after it has executed complete save function. so, if we add check `has_changed(field)` to all four instances, all four instances will return `True` (of course, when there has been relevant change)

Alright, I understand that use case, but that's not the use case I was facing: the code I was optimising involved a tree of queries through some disparate code paths where some of the leaves overlap, so `select_related` / `prefetch_related` was not the applicable optimisation.

The duplicate detection by SQL query alone was not useful for this: it detects all queries for the same type as duplicates, even when they're from completely different places in the code, and are intended that way. What I was interested in was only the relatively small proportion of duplicate queries for the same objects: these are the code paths where a little bit of transient caching would avoid a lot of redundant database traffic. Adjusting the duplicate detection made a big difference in usability: it highlights exactly the 2 or 3 relevant code paths, versus having to comb through 10 or 20 detected duplicates that are unrelated.

I would guess I'm not the only one who'd benefit from this view: is there a sensible way to provide for both use cases? I can think of the following options:

1. Allow some kind of setting to control this behaviour. (Inconvenient, not very discoverable.)
2. Allow an arbitrary developer-specified `duplicate_key` function. (Probably overkill, and too exposing of internals?)
3. Track and report both by default. (Most developer-friendly?)

I'm leaning toward option 3: it shouldn't be too hard to implement.

In the report, the wording that would make sense for me would be to report the current number (ignoring params) as "similar queries", and exact duplicates (including params) as "duplicate queries". So, a query that currently reports 20 duplicates may instead report "20 similar, 3 duplicates".

Do you have thoughts or feedback on this?
