### PROP 112: PROP_STREAM_DEBUG {#prop-stream-debug}

* Type: Read-Only-Stream
* Packed-Encoding: `D`

Octets: |    *n*
--------|------------
Fields: | UTF8_DATA

This property is a streaming property, meaning that you cannot explicitly
fetch the value of this property. The stream provides human-readable debugging
output which may be displayed in the host logs.

The location of newline characters is not assumed by the host: it is
the NCP's responsibility to insert newline characters where needed,
just like with any other text stream.

To receive the debugging stream, you wait for `CMD_PROP_VALUE_IS`
commands for this property from the NCP.
```

How about adding a "expectedResponseTimeout" (exRT) field to otMessageInfo? By default, 0 would mean that no response is expected, and non-zero values - to expect a response in no more than exRT. MAC layer would then send N data requests every exRT/N milliseconds. Or instead of linear delay between data requests there could be an exponential delay like in CSMA.
Also, if multiple messages with exRT!=0 were sent (e.g. from MLE and app layer), then the MAC could use some intermediate values, so that the device would not spam too many data requests.

@kmvanbrunt @MarkLalor 
I think you both have a valid perspective and points in regards to the ``default_to_shell`` flag.  In summary:

- Advantage:  It makes it extremely easy for developers to create an application based on ``cmd2`` which contains shell-like functionality
- Disadvantage:  It complicates the maintenance of ``cmd2``, perhaps unnecessarily

My opinion is that it is probably best to move forward with this PR containing improvements to the existing feature and preserve backward compatibility at least for now. 

Once the ``alias`` feature is complete and we have all had time to experiment with it, then maybe it would be good to re-open the discussion about the ``default_to_shell`` flag.

Possibly of relevance, I've thought for awhile now that we may need to effectively override the default manner in which commands using one of the two ``cmd2`` **argparse** decorators display their help.

Currently this is purely delegated to ``argparse``.  And there is a cheap hack within ``cmd2.Cmd.do_help``  to temporarily redirect both ``sys.stdout`` and ``sys.stderr`` while ``argparse`` is doing its thing for displaying the help.

Previously when we had the ``optparse`` decorator, there was a class which overrode the default ``optparse`` function for displaying help.  I think we might need to do something similar for ``argparse``.

I can think of several ways we could make this happen:

1. Pass `do_` methods a list of arguments instead of a string. Very `sys.argv`-ish. If we didn't have to worry about backwards compatibility, this makes the most sense to me. But we do have to worry about backwards compatibility, so I don't this this approach is viable.
2. Use @tleonhardt's suggestion to use @options or the new @with_argument_parser decorators. They both shlex the input. If we like this method, all we have to change is the documentation. But it's not as convenient as it could be.
3. Create a decorator which you can apply to `do_` methods which indicates to pass a list of arguments instead of a string. Works, but the decorator would have to be applied to every `do_` method. Makes it easy to migrate to this new method, because you can migrate one method at a time.
4. Create a new subclass of `str` which has a `arglist()` method. Pass an instance of this new class to the `do_` methods where we are currently passing a plain string. Existing code works fine, if you want a list of arguments, call the `arglist()` method.
5. Create a new attribute on the `cmd()` class, similar to `blankLinesAllowed` or `case_insensitive` called `quoted_arguments`, which defaults to False, but if set True makes `cmd` pass a list of arguments to `do_` methods, instead of a string.

I think a combination of method 3 and method 5 would be very convenient. For new code you could use method 5. To migrate existing code, you could use method 3 during the migration. When you have changed all of your `do_` methods, you use method 5 and delete all your method 3 decorators.

What do you think?

Thanks for the extensive writeup!

I have many thoughts, but perhaps the most important is in response to this:
> Some new users think that "renew" does not replace the certificate, just somehow automagically extend the validity of the existing certificate.

This is an intentional design choice. Our goal is to create an abstraction, so that most users don't need to know that `renew` does not automagically extend the validity of the existing certificate.

With that out of the way, on more substantive topics:

- I like the `--add-domain` and `--remove-domain` flags! That's a great idea. It follows clearly from the `--cert-name`/`--lineage` flag, and you make a fantastic point about computers being better able to copy over domain names than humans can.
- How would you imagine these flags affecting certonly/run commands? At first glance those don't appear to be related, but if you request an existing cert name that isn't close to expiry, we catch that and ask if you'd like to not get a new cert just yet.
- Do you imagine that modify is an exact stand-in for renew? If a cert isn't due for renewal, should calling modify get a new cert anyway?
- On the topic of deprecation, we are more likely to hide things from the help interface than to actually deprecate them -- people script things in their cron jobs and never look at them again, and we can't break that. We also have requirements to our distributors to keep existing workflows stable.

We can look into trying to do something like this, but to be honest, we probably won't get to it for a while. If you or someone else would like to take a crack at fixing it, feel free.

The good news though is now that you have the initial setup, if you renew your certificate with `certbot renew` (which is the recommended way to do so), we won't modify your config file again.
Note: remember to test downgrading Certbot versions

I think currently Ray is still lack of a good resource management ability.
For GCS, its memory is never recycled.
For object store, it simply adopts LRU-based eviction now, which definitely isn't the most effective approach.

Thus, I think we can consider introducing a Resource Manager component into the core. It takes responsibility for managing the life cycle of all resources in the system, including GCS, object store, etc.

I haven't thought about the detailed design of the Resource Manager. But I think having a component that unifies resource management will be great. 

Also, I'd like to hear @concretevitamin's plan to address GCS memory issue.

Thanks for the bug report.

So the original problem was solved by downgrading to `0.3.0`? Is `0.3.1` still not working for you? The version string problem should be fixed in the current master.

Ok, after more investigation, the problem appears to be the following.

The problem is easily reproducible about 75% of the time on 100 m4.xlarge nodes by doing the following

```python
import ray

ray.init(redis_address=...)

@ray.remote
def f():
    return 1

l = ray.get([f.remote() for _ in range(1000)])
```

The problem is with the call to `connect`, which is called by `connect_inet_sock` in `io.cc`, which is called by `get_manager_connection` in `plasma_manager.cc`. **This call takes around 15 seconds on some machines.** Since the monitor process doesn't receive any heartbeats for 10 seconds, it marks the plasma manager as dead.

The relevant line is here https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/8d350f628ad35aead18523db90abe840d7edfca3/src/common/io.cc#L213, which is called from here https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/8d350f628ad35aead18523db90abe840d7edfca3/src/plasma/plasma_manager.cc#L804.

@mitar Hi Mitar, I don't think calling `logging. basicConfig` is a good practice. For one thing, `logging. basicConfig` should be used only once. When you call it the second time, it changes nothing. For another, once there is a default logger, when a user tries to create a new logger with a new handler, there will be duplicated messages. https://github.com/Theano/Theano/issues/1815 .

Anyway, removing the default logger and using `__name__` are good advices.

Current implementation `LocalizedUniqueSlugField` with `AtomicSlugRetryMixin` has some problems:
 - Conceptual. For use only field we need modify model, it is conceptual not properly. Model is model, field is field if we add field to model we don't must modify model class for this. IMHO
 - Code sharing. Code splits in two part `LocalizedUniqueSlugField.pre_save` and `AtomicSlugRetryMixin.save`. AtomicSlugRetryMixin define `retries`, but it is uses in `LocalizedUniqueSlugField`. I open SSH tunnels to all of my bastions (I use a One Time Password token). 
When I SSH to a host, ~/.ssh/config is parsed and matches rules based on the hostmask.

lets say my server list is:
server1.usa.example.com
server2.usa.example.com
server1.can.example.com
server2.can.example.com
server1.mex.example.com
server2.mex.example.com


~/.ssh/config:
# USA Bastion
Host bastion1.usa.example.com
Host server*.usa.example.com
  ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p bastion1.usa.example.com

# Canada Bastion
Host bastion1.can.example.com
Host server*.can.example.com
  ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p bastion1.can.example.com

# Mexico Bastion
Host bastion1.mex.example.com
Host server*.mex.example.com
  ProxyCommand ssh -W %h:%p bastion1.mex.example.com

### Attempt 1, Hashing approach
Start by observing that we can make a blocking rule that allows blocks together nearby integers, by creating block_keys that range around the focal integer. Say we have records the contain the age of persons, and we want to compare people who have recorded ages within 2 years of one another. We can do this by taking each age and making a block keys for each number in the range 

`[focal age - 2, focal age + 2]`

So, we have the following three records

```
record_id,age
1,25
2,30
3,32
```

this would generate blocking like 

```
block_key,record_id
23,1
24,1
25,1
26,1
27,1
28,2
29,2
30,2
31,3
32,3
30,3,
31,3
32,3
33,3
34,3
```
records 2 and 3 have overlapping block keys so we will compare those records. We won't compare 1 and 2 or 1 and 3 because there are no overlapping block keys.

Next, observe that for a given set **A** with cardinality N then the smallest possible set **B** that could have a particular Jaccard Index value would have cardinality  ```ceiling(N * (Jaccard Index value)```, and the largest set could have cardinality ```floor(N / (Jaccard Index value)```.

For example, say we have a set **A** of 4 elements and we have a desired Jaccard Index value of 0.5. The smallest set that could do that would have 2 elements and the largest set could be have 8 elements.

At the time of blocking, we know how many block_keys are created by a block predicate. We can use these relationships above to create compound blocks to filter out record pairs that could not have the desired minimum Jaccard Index value.

So say we have a tokenizing predicate that generates the following block_keys

```
{'the', 'lazy', 'fox', 'jumped'}
```

We'd like to only compare only compare records where the Jaccard Index of the tokens is 0.5 or greater.

We can then create the following block_keys:

```
{'the:2', 'lazy:2', 'fox:2', 'jumped:2',
 'the:3', 'lazy:3', 'fox:3', 'jumped:3',
 'the:4', 'lazy:4', 'fox:4', 'jumped:4',
 'the:5', 'lazy:5', 'fox:5', 'jumped:5',
 'the:6', 'lazy:6', 'fox:6', 'jumped:6',
 'the:7', 'lazy:7', 'fox:7', 'jumped:7',
 'the:8', 'lazy:8', 'fox:8', 'jumped:8'}
```

However, using the multiple matching scheme described above, we can do better than this and vary the number of required matches before comparison. Again, assuming we want a Jaccard Index of 0.5, the required matches are expressed by the following relation

```
minimum_required_matches = floor((Jaccard Index)/(1 + Jaccard Index)(|A| + |B|))
```

Here again are the block keys along with associated minimum matches to get a Jaccard index of 0.5

```
{('the:2', 2), ('lazy:2', 2), ('fox:2', 2), ('jumped:2', 2),
 ('the:3', 3), ('lazy:3', 3), ('fox:3', 3), ('jumped:3', 3),
 ('the:4', 3), ('lazy:4', 3), ('fox:4', 3), ('jumped:4', 3),
 ('the:5', 3), ('lazy:5', 3), ('fox:5', 3), ('jumped:5', 3),
 ('the:6', 4), ('lazy:6', 4), ('fox:6', 4), ('jumped:6', 4),
 ('the:7', 4), ('lazy:7', 4), ('fox:7', 4), ('jumped:7', 4)
 ('the:8', 4), ('lazy:8', 4), ('fox:8', 4), ('jumped:8', 4)}
```

This approach works, but as is probably evident it produced an enormous multiplication of block keys and blocks. As such, it does not seems currently practical for cases where there will be more than three or four original block keys. I have a rough implementation here: https://github.com/dedupeio/dedupe/tree/jaccard_hashing

import jpype

	jpype.attachThreadToJVM()

Yes, one of the uglier aspects of virtualenv's implementation is that it has to have its own copy of the site module, which is used for all virtualenvs regardless of which version of Python they are created with. And this copy predates the addition of `site.getsitepackages()` (first added in Python 2.7, I believe).

I would have no objection to a pull request adding that function to virtualenv's site module, as long as the implementation is one that can work on all virtualenv-supported versions of Python (2.4 through 3.3a). Or if that's too difficult due to missing APIs in other areas of the earlier Python versions, perhaps have it conditionally defined depending on `sys.version` or something.
How's that look?
